name: Planet Labs PBC

profile.description: Planet Labs operates the world's largest commercial Earth observation satellite constellation with 200+ satellites, providing daily global imaging data to governments, agriculture, forestry, and financial intelligence customers. The company achieved its first full-year adjusted EBITDA profitability in FY2026 (ended January 2026), with total revenue of $307.7M (+26% YoY) and Q4 revenue surging 41% YoY to $86.8M. Planet is structured as a Delaware Public Benefit Corporation.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track record (3/3): Planet pioneered the small-satellite constellation model for daily global imaging, successfully pivoted from satellite manufacturer to AI-powered data-intelligence platform, and achieved first full-year EBITDA profitability in FY2026 with revenue accelerating from 10% growth in Q1 to 41% in Q4 — a rare trajectory shift [1][2]. AI deployment (2/2): Planet is shipping AI-powered analytics products including crop health monitoring, deforestation detection, and maritime vessel tracking; CEO Will Marshall has stated AI will 'change the status quo' for extracting information from satellite imagery, and the company's platform now enables automated change detection and anomaly identification [3]. Market share (2/2): Planet operates the world's largest commercial Earth observation constellation with 200+ satellites providing daily global coverage — category leader in revisit rate and geographic breadth [2]. Pipeline (1/2): FY2027 guidance of $415–$440M (+35-43% YoY) with $900M total backlog (79% growth) provides strong visibility, though specific next-gen satellite timelines are less defined [1]. R&D intensity (0/1): R&D spend of $107M in FY2026 represents ~35% of revenue, well above aerospace peers, but much of this is capitalized in satellite operations [1].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission floor (3/4): Planet's mission — 'using space to help life on Earth' — is specific, service-oriented, and validated by a decade of consistent execution building the world's largest Earth imaging constellation; it connects commercial satellite operations to humanitarian outcomes (disaster response, climate monitoring, food security) in a way that differentiates from peers [2][4]. Community impact (1/2): Planet provides data at reduced rates to NGOs and researchers for climate monitoring, disaster response, and cultural heritage protection (e.g., Ukraine cultural heritage work with Global Heritage Fund) [4]. Employee retention (1/2): the 945-person team has been maintained through SPAC-era turbulence and cost restructuring; founder-led leadership under Will Marshall since 2010 provides continuity [2][3]. Insider buying (1/2): management executed on the long-term profitability promise, but specific recent insider purchase data was not identified in research [1].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Growth sleeve. Liquidity check: $42.66 × 12.9M avg volume = ~$551M daily, well above $1M threshold. D/E 245.4% scores 0.24 (target ≤70%, floor 300%). ROE -78.4% scores 0.0 (2x weight). Revenue growth YoY 41.1% scores 1.0 (target ≥10%). Revenue 5yr CAGR ~17.2% scores 1.0 (2x weight). Forward P/E negative (unprofitable) scores 0.0. Profit margin -80.2% scores 0.0. PEG ratio unavailable, excluded. Weighted sum: 0.24×1 + 0×2 + 1.0×1 + 1.0×2 + 0×1 + 0×1 = 3.24 / 8 max weight = 0.40 → infrastructure = 4 [1].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Planet Labs is structurally immune to AI disruption — the company owns the physical satellite constellation that generates the proprietary daily imagery dataset AI models require. No amount of AI advancement can replicate the physical infrastructure needed to image the entire Earth daily; AI actually increases demand for Planet's data as more organizations deploy geospatial AI analytics [3]. The Defense & Intelligence segment (50%+ revenue growth in FY2026) benefits directly from AI-driven intelligence demand [1]. AI Opportunity (1/1): Planet generates quantifiable AI revenue through its analytics platform, which applies computer vision and machine learning to satellite imagery for automated crop monitoring, deforestation alerts, and maritime tracking — these AI-powered products are core to the company's data-intelligence pivot [3].

infinite_game.summary: Planet Labs exemplifies infinite game thinking through a decade-long commitment to daily Earth imaging despite years of operating losses. The company maintained its mission through SPAC-era volatility, cost restructurings, and the transition from satellite builder to data platform. The FY2026 EBITDA profitability milestone validates the long game — achieved under the same founder-CEO who articulated the vision in 2010.

just_cause.analysis: Planet's Just Cause — 'using space to help life on Earth' — is transformative, inclusive, and validated by 10+ years of consistent execution. The mission has survived funding crunches, a SPAC listing, and industry skepticism. Strategic decisions (building the largest constellation, pivoting to data platform, serving humanitarian use cases) trace directly back to this purpose. It falls just short of a 10 because the cause hasn't yet been tested by a leadership transition [2][4]. (2 pts)

trusting_teams.analysis: Planet's workforce has navigated from startup to profitability under consistent founder leadership. The company executed cost restructuring ('hard changes including cuts to costs and teams') while maintaining mission focus, suggesting adequate organizational trust. However, workforce reductions and the lack of standout retention data prevent a higher score [2][3]. (1 pt)

worthy_rivals.analysis: Planet positions against Maxar (high-resolution defense imaging), BlackSky, Satellogic, and Airbus Defence as worthy rivals whose capabilities in SAR and high-resolution imaging push Planet to improve its revisit rate, analytics depth, and spectral capabilities (Tanager hyperspectral satellite). The competitive framing is improvement-oriented rather than zero-sum [2]. (1 pt)

existential_flexibility.analysis: Planet's pivot from satellite hardware manufacturer to AI-powered data-intelligence platform represents genuine existential flexibility. The company recognized that long-term value lies in the analytics layer built on proprietary daily imagery — not in the satellites themselves. This mirrors Fujifilm's transformation from film to materials science, driven by mission rather than desperation [1][3]. (2 pts)

courage_to_lead.analysis: Will Marshall sustained investment in constellation deployment and data infrastructure through years of cumulative losses exceeding $500M, maintaining the vision when investors pressured for near-term profitability. The decision to restructure costs while continuing satellite launches demonstrated choosing long-term capability over short-term comfort. However, the company has not yet made a truly transformative asymmetric bet beyond its core thesis [1][2]. (1 pt)

investment_thesis: Planet Labs hit a fundamental inflection in FY2026 — first full-year adjusted EBITDA profitability ($15.5M) on $307.7M revenue (+26% YoY), with Q4 revenue accelerating to $86.8M (+41% YoY). The company's $900M total backlog (+79% YoY) and FY2027 guidance of $415–$440M provide strong forward visibility [1]. The data-intelligence pivot — AI analytics layered on the world's largest daily Earth imagery dataset — addresses expanding intelligence demand across defense (50%+ growth), agriculture, climate, and financial services. As a Delaware Public Benefit Corporation with a structurally irreplaceable physical data moat, Planet is uniquely positioned at the intersection of space, AI, and sustainability.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on persistent GAAP losses (-$246.9M net loss in FY2026, worse than FY2025's -$123.2M) driven by stock-based compensation and satellite depreciation, plus elevated debt (D/E 245%) [1]. The stock has surged ~58% from $27 to $43, pricing in much of the growth acceleration. Competition from SAR providers (Capella Space, Umbra) offering all-weather imaging and high-resolution optical competitors (Maxar) could pressure government contract margins. GAAP profitability remains distant, and the company must sustain 30%+ revenue growth to justify current valuations.
key_catalysts: FY2027 revenue hitting $415M+ would confirm the data-intelligence business model is scaling and could drive re-rating toward software/data company multiples
First full-year GAAP profitability would remove the final financial overhang and unlock institutional mandates requiring profitable holdings
New multi-year government intelligence contracts (US DoD or allied nations) at $50M+ annual value would validate Planet as a strategic defense intelligence partner
key_risks: Government budget concentration: a significant portion of revenue comes from US and allied defense/intelligence contracts; budget sequestration or program cuts would impair near-term growth

Persistent GAAP losses: net loss widened to -$247M in FY2026 from -$123M, driven by SBC and depreciation; inability to reach GAAP profitability could erode investor patience at current elevated valuations
Satellite replenishment and competition: maintaining 200+ satellites requires continuous launches while SAR competitors (Capella, Umbra) offer all-weather imaging that optical systems cannot match
sources: Planet Labs FY2026 Annual Earnings and Q4 Results, March 2026
Planet Labs corporate website — planet.com/company, accessed May 2026
Will Marshall interview with Axios on AI and satellite imagery, November 2025; Yahoo Finance CEO interview on AI data centers
Planet Labs ESG Report 2023 and Public Benefit Corporation filings
